seo.page_title: Hyundai Ioniq 5 vs Kia EV6 import to NZ
seo.meta_description: Compare Hyundai Ioniq 5 vs Kia EV6 for NZ import. Battery health, real-world range, charging and landed costs when buying from Japan.
seo.h1_tag: Hyundai Ioniq 5 vs Kia EV6 for NZ
seo.og_title: Hyundai Ioniq 5 vs Kia EV6 — NZ import guide
seo.og_description: Side-by-side look at Hyundai Ioniq 5 and Kia EV6 for NZ buyers. Battery health, range, charging and what to know when importing from Japan.
seo.primary_keyword: Hyundai Ioniq 5 vs Kia EV6 NZ

blocks.hero_h1: Hyundai Ioniq 5 vs Kia EV6 for NZ
blocks.hero_subtitle: A clear, NZ-focused comparison of Hyundai Ioniq 5 and Kia EV6 when importing from Japan, with an emphasis on battery health, real-world range and charging that suits local driving.
proof_points: Transparent pricing — compliance is part of the process
blocks.why_kiwis_buy: Hyundai Ioniq 5 and Kia EV6 both offer long-range EV motoring with space for family life and weekend trips. They suit mixed driving — city commuting during the week and open-road runs on the weekend — while keeping running costs predictable. Importing from Japan opens up more battery sizes and trim levels than you’ll usually see on local yards.

savings_block.headline: What you can typically save vs NZ dealer stock
savings_block.body: Buying a Hyundai Ioniq 5 or Kia EV6 through the Japanese auctions often means more choice on battery size and trim for similar or lower landed cost than many NZ yards. Your total spend includes the auction price, shipping, insurance, NZ entry certification, on-road costs and our fee. We lay out each part so you can compare against local asking prices with real numbers.

cta_form.headline: Find your Ioniq 5 or EV6 — we'll do the searching
cta_form.subtext: Fill in your details to get started. We'll search the Japanese auctions and come back with real options and pricing by email.

blocks.video_caption: See how the flat floor and wide cabin in the Ioniq 5 and EV6 work in real life, from rear legroom to how easily you can load bikes or prams.
blocks.main_usp_deep: Hyundai Ioniq 5 and Kia EV6 share the same EV platform, so you’re choosing between two well-sorted options rather than a good and a bad one. The Ioniq 5 leans more towards family practicality with its boxier shape and easier loading, while the EV6 feels more driver-focused with a lower, sportier stance. When we shortlist cars in Japan, we pay close attention to battery health records, odometer readings and any fast-charging history noted. That way you can weigh up range, spec and budget with real information rather than guesswork.

buyer_persona.headline: Is this car right for you?
buyer_persona.body: You’re comparing Hyundai Ioniq 5 and Kia EV6 because you want a proper EV that can handle school runs, commuting and longer trips without constant charging stops. You’re probably weighing cabin space, tech features and how each one will fit your driveway and lifestyle. If you like clear trade-offs laid out before you decide, this comparison is a solid match.

trust_company.headline: Why import with ImportYourCar.co.nz?
trust_company.body: We’re an NZ-based team that’s been importing vehicles from Japan since 2009, including a growing number of EVs like the Hyundai Ioniq 5 and Kia EV6. We give you full auction documentation, photos and our own notes before you decide to bid. You see the real landed cost breakdown, including shipping and NZ entry certification, before committing. Our role is to help you balance budget, condition and spec so the car you land in NZ matches what you expected.

car_specs_benefits.spec: Shared EV platform (Hyundai Ioniq 5 and Kia EV6)
car_specs_benefits.benefit: Parts, servicing knowledge and driving feel are broadly similar, which helps long-term ownership in NZ.

car_specs_benefits.spec: Multiple battery sizes available in Japan market
car_specs_benefits.benefit: You can target a battery that matches your daily driving and budget, rather than taking whatever is on a local yard.

car_specs_benefits.spec: Spacious cabin layouts in both models
car_specs_benefits.benefit: Comfortable for family use, car seats and taller drivers on longer NZ trips.
Makes better use of NZ’s growing DC charging network on intercity runs.

car_specs_benefits.spec: Modern safety and driver-assist tech (varies by trim)
car_specs_benefits.benefit: Helps with long days on the road and busy urban driving, especially if you’re stepping up from an older car.

cta_mid_page.headline: Tell us how you drive — we’ll match you to Ioniq 5 or EV6 options
cta_mid_page.subtext: Share your budget, range needs and preferred spec, and we’ll come back with real cars from the Japanese auctions.

models_comparison.intro: Here’s how Hyundai Ioniq 5 and Kia EV6 imports typically compare for NZ buyers.

rows.notes: Both models are recent, so they generally meet current NZ safety expectations. Later years may bring software updates and small feature changes, which can matter if you want the latest driver-assist tech.

Japan-market cars offer different battery sizes and motor outputs across Ioniq 5 and EV6. Larger batteries suit longer NZ trips but cost more; smaller packs can work well if you mostly drive in town and charge at home.
Higher trims can add features like upgraded audio, more driver-assist functions and different interior finishes. We flag these on each auction listing so you can see what you’re paying for.
As late-model EVs, they typically meet NZ entry standards if documentation is clean and no modifications are noted. We still need to see the car in NZ before entry certification is confirmed.

Both Ioniq 5 and EV6 appear regularly at major Japanese auctions, but specific trims and colours can be less common. That availability affects how long it might take to find the right one at your budget.

steps.title: Tell us what you're after
steps.body: Fill in the form or message us. We confirm your brief and give you a cost breakdown for Hyundai Ioniq 5 and Kia EV6 options before anything moves forward.

steps.title: We search the auctions
steps.body: We monitor USS, TAA, HAA and others. For EVs like the Ioniq 5 and EV6, we check battery health records and note any fast-charging history on each shortlisted car.

steps.title: You approve, we bid
steps.body: We send you the auction sheet, photos and our recommendation. Once you're happy, you pay a deposit and we bid on your behalf.

steps.title: Shipping to NZ
steps.body: Typically 2–4 weeks depending on the sailing schedule. For less common trims or colours, allow a little extra time once the auction clears. We keep you updated along the way.

steps.title: Compliance and delivery
steps.body: The car goes through NZ entry certification and compliance. Once it's through, we arrange delivery or you can collect.

risks_solutions.intro: Importing an EV is straightforward, but it helps to understand the main risks and how we handle them.

items.risk: Worrying about battery health on a used Ioniq 5 or EV6 from Japan.
items.solution: We focus on auction listings with clear battery information and service records where available, and avoid cars with obvious red flags like very high fast-charging counts or unclear history.

items.risk: Concern about hidden costs on top of the auction price.
items.solution: We provide a landed cost estimate that separates auction price, shipping, insurance, NZ entry certification, on-road costs and our fee so you can see where your money goes.

items.risk: Uncertainty about auction grades and what they mean for condition.
items.solution: We explain the auction grade, translate key parts of the sheet and add our own notes on visible wear, interior condition and any recorded repairs before you decide to bid.

blocks.how_to_use_iyc: Tell us whether you’re leaning towards Hyundai Ioniq 5, Kia EV6 or open to both, plus your budget and range needs. We’ll shortlist suitable cars from the Japanese auctions, explain the pros and cons of each, and you choose what to bid on. If you’d like to pause or wait for a better match, that’s fine too.

faq_block.q: Is the Hyundai Ioniq 5 or Kia EV6 better for NZ driving?
faq_block.a: They share a lot under the skin, so it mostly comes down to shape and feel. The Ioniq 5 is a bit more upright and family-focused, while the EV6 feels sportier. We can talk through how you drive and suggest which is likely to suit you more.

faq_block.q: How much does it cost to import an Ioniq 5 or EV6 from Japan?
faq_block.a: Total cost depends on battery size, trim, year and auction price on the day. On top of the auction price you’ll have shipping, insurance, NZ entry certification, on-road costs and our fee. We give you a clear estimate before you commit to bidding.

faq_block.q: Do these models typically meet NZ entry standards?
faq_block.a: As late-model EVs, Hyundai Ioniq 5 and Kia EV6 typically meet NZ entry standards if documentation is clean and no modifications are noted. Final entry certification can only be confirmed after inspection in NZ.

faq_block.q: How long will it take to get my imported Ioniq 5 or EV6?
faq_block.a: Once we win an auction, shipping to NZ is usually around 2–4 weeks, plus time for clearance and entry certification. Finding the right car at auction can be quick or take a few weeks depending on your budget and spec requirements.

faq_block.q: What auction grade should I look for on an Ioniq 5 or EV6?
faq_block.a: Most buyers aim for mid to higher grades that balance condition and price. We explain what each grade means, highlight any repair notes and help you decide whether a particular car is worth bidding on.

footer_cta.headline: Ready to compare real Ioniq 5 and EV6 options?
footer_cta.subtext: Tell us your budget, range needs and preferred spec, and we’ll send through live Japanese auction options with full landed cost estimates.

subscription_block.headline: Stay in the loop
subscription_block.subtext: Get updates on Hyundai Ioniq 5 and Kia EV6 auction listings, price trends and practical EV import tips for NZ.

blocks.company_positioning: ImportYourCar.co.nz is an NZ-based vehicle import service working directly with Japanese auctions since 2009. We handle a wide range of vehicles, including EVs such as the Hyundai Ioniq 5 and Kia EV6. Our focus is on clear information, full documentation and realistic landed cost estimates so you can make an informed decision.

landed_cost_breakdown.intro: Your landed cost is the total to get a Hyundai Ioniq 5 or Kia EV6 from a Japanese auction onto NZ roads.

rows.notes: What the car sells for at the Japanese auction, plus local auction fees.
Transport from the Japanese port to NZ and cover for the vehicle while it’s on the water.
Inspection, paperwork and any work needed so the car can be registered and driven legally in NZ.

auction_checklist.check: Battery health information and any recorded checks
auction_checklist.why: Gives you a sense of remaining capacity and likely real-world range in NZ conditions.
Higher mileage and heavy fast-charging use can affect long-term battery performance.
Shows overall condition and whether the car has had significant repairs that might matter later.
Helps spot wear, scuffs or modifications that may not be obvious from the grade alone.

auction_checklist.check: Charging port type and included charging cables
auction_checklist.why: Ensures the car will work smoothly with NZ charging infrastructure and your home setup.

not_for_you_block.headline: Who this may not suit
items: You want a very low upfront price and aren’t concerned about battery health or long-term range.
You need a seven-seat vehicle or a people mover layout.
You’re not comfortable with a bit of waiting and uncertainty around auction outcomes and shipping timelines.

alternatives_block.intro: If neither Hyundai Ioniq 5 nor Kia EV6 feels quite right, there are other EVs worth considering.

rows.better_if: You want another modern EV with a slightly different design and interior feel.
rows.tradeoff: Availability at auction can be more limited, and local support is still developing compared with more established models.

battery_health_block.headline: Battery health on Ioniq 5 and EV6 imports
battery_health_block.body: Battery condition is central to choosing between Hyundai Ioniq 5 and Kia EV6 from Japan. While these are relatively new models, usage patterns and fast-charging habits still matter. We focus on listings with clear service records or battery checks where available, and avoid cars with obvious warning signs.
checklist: Look for documented battery checks or service records in the auction notes.
Consider odometer reading alongside age to understand how heavily the car has been used.
Ask about fast-charging history if it’s mentioned, as heavy DC use can affect long-term capacity.
Be open to slightly higher mileage cars if the price reflects it and the battery history looks sensible.

charging_block.headline: Charging Hyundai Ioniq 5 and Kia EV6 in NZ
charging_block.home_charging: Most Ioniq 5 and EV6 owners in NZ rely on overnight home charging. A dedicated wall unit makes the most sense, but even a standard socket can work if your daily driving is modest and you plan ahead.
charging_block.public_charging: Both models are well suited to NZ’s public charging network, with fast-charging capability on most trims. That makes intercity trips manageable, especially if you plan stops around known DC sites.
caveats: Check the charging port type on the specific Japan-market car to confirm it matches what you expect in NZ.
If you do frequent long trips, focus on trims with faster DC charging capability and larger batteries.
Some imported cables may not be ideal for NZ sockets, so you may want to budget for a local charging solution.

range_real_world_block.headline: Real-world range for NZ roads
range_real_world_block.body: Official range figures are one thing; how far you actually get on NZ roads is another. For both Hyundai Ioniq 5 and Kia EV6, real-world range depends on battery size, driving style, terrain and how much time you spend at motorway speeds. We help you interpret overseas figures so you can pick a battery that comfortably covers your daily use with a buffer for bad weather and detours.

ev_running_costs_block.headline: Running costs for Ioniq 5 and EV6 in NZ
ev_running_costs_block.body: Day-to-day running costs for Hyundai Ioniq 5 and Kia EV6 are usually lower than similar-sized non-EV vehicles, especially if you can charge at home on a good power plan. You’ll still need to budget for tyres, servicing of non-battery components and any software or navigation updates you want. We can give you a rough idea of likely annual costs based on how and where you drive.

which_one_to_choose_block.headline: Should you choose Ioniq 5 or EV6?
which_one_to_choose_block.body: If you prioritise easy loading, a more upright driving position and family practicality, the Hyundai Ioniq 5 often makes more sense. If you care more about a sportier feel and slightly lower stance, the Kia EV6 may appeal more. In both cases, battery size, trim and condition matter as much as the badge, so we’ll help you compare real cars rather than just spec sheets.

budget_bands_block.headline: How budget affects your Ioniq 5 vs EV6 choice
budget_bands_block.body: At lower budgets you may be looking at earlier years, smaller batteries or simpler trims of Ioniq 5 and EV6. As your budget rises, you can usually target larger batteries, higher trims and lower mileage. We’ll talk through what’s realistic in your price range and whether one model tends to offer better value than the other at that level.
